{{Chess Position|= |Panteldakis Countergambit| |rd|nd|bd|qd|kd|bd|nd|rd|= |pd|pd|pd|pd| | |pd|pd|= | | | | | | | | |= | | | | |pd|pd| | |= | | | | |pl|pl| | |= | | | | | | | | |= |pl|pl|pl|pl| | |pl|pl|= |rl|nl|bl|ql|kl|bl|nl|rl|= |parent=[[Chess/Open Game|Open Game]] |moves=1.e4 e5 2.f4 f5 |eco=[[Chess/ECOC|C30]] }} = Panteldakis Countergambit = Among the oldest countergambits in the King's Gambit Declined is the Panteldakis Countergambit, 1.e4 e5 2.f4 f5?!, known from a game played in 1625 in which Gioachino Greco used it to win with the Black pieces. It is nonetheless considered dubious because 3. exf5 with the threat of Qh5+ gives White a good game. ==Theory table== {{Chess Opening Theory/Table}} '''1.e4 e5 2.f4 f5'''
